questions.scenario: `app-orders-prod` has a system-assigned managed identity. Key Vault still returns `Forbidden`. What does this prove? The app starts successfully, and the managed identity object ID appears in the resource overview, but the secret read still fails.

options.text: The app has a caller identity, but that identity still needs the right Key Vault permission or access model.
Managed identity failed to exist, because Key Vault access is automatic once identity is enabled.
The app should switch to a user-assigned identity because system-assigned identities cannot read secrets.
Key Vault cannot be used by Azure-hosted apps without storing a client secret.

questions.explanation: Identity answers who the app is. Authorization answers what that identity can do and where. Enabling identity alone does not grant Key Vault access.

questions.scenario: The app can read Key Vault but cannot write to Blob Storage. Both calls use the same managed identity. What is the best interpretation? The logs show a successful `GET secret` call followed by a failed `Put Blob` call from the same running revision.

options.text: The identity has one permission path working, but storage write access is separate and must be checked at the storage scope.
The identity is broken because a working identity should have access to every Azure service.
The storage account should copy Key Vault role assignments automatically after the first successful secret read.
The app needs a second identity for every Azure service, because one identity cannot have several roles.

questions.explanation: One identity can have different permissions at different scopes. A successful Key Vault read does not imply Blob write permission.

questions.scenario: A user-assigned identity `mi-orders-runtime-prod` is attached to both the API and a background worker. The worker needs Blob write access, but the API only needs Blob read access. What should the team consider?

options.text: Whether sharing one identity gives the API more storage power than it needs, and whether separate identities would reduce blast radius.
Whether the API can ignore RBAC because the worker already has the write role.
Whether a system-assigned identity would automatically split read and write actions for each app.
Whether Blob Storage can identify callers by container image tag instead of identity.

questions.explanation: A shared identity means shared permissions. If two workloads need different power, separate identities can make least privilege clearer. Identity type alone does not automatically split permission behavior.

questions.scenario: Which records help prove a managed identity setup is ready for release? The release reviewer wants proof that the production app itself, not a developer laptop or pipeline precheck, has the right access.

options.text: The app or workload that has the identity attached.
The identity object ID or client ID used by the runtime.
The role assignments or access policy at each target resource.
The developer's local Azure CLI account that tested the feature.

questions.explanation: A release review needs workload, identity, and target permissions. A developer's local account is not proof that the runtime identity works.

questions.scenario: A teammate wants to grant `Contributor` at resource-group scope to a managed identity because the app needs to read one secret and write one blob container. What is the safer design review comment?

options.text: Grant the specific secret-read and blob-write permissions needed at the smallest useful scopes instead of general Contributor.
Contributor is safer than data roles because it avoids thinking about each target resource.
The app needs Owner instead, because Contributor does not include every possible emergency action.
The app should use a human account because managed identity RBAC is too narrow for production.

questions.explanation: The app has two specific jobs. The permissions should match those jobs. Broad management roles are easier to request but create unnecessary power.
